title: Manage and Summarize Documents
content:
Document dashboard After authentication, the SmartStudy dashboard displays the user’s document library and processing state. The interface provides document statistics, search, upload access, and navigation to the study room and practice features.
Figure 5.25: SmartStudy dashboard with ready documents in the personal library.
Document management API Protected document operations use a Bearer access token:
GET /api/v1/documents lists documents and supports search, status, page, and limit parameters. GET /api/v1/documents/{documentId} returns metadata, page count, and detected chapters. DELETE /api/v1/documents/{documentId} removes the stored object and soft-deletes the document record. Figure 5.26: Authenticated API operations for document management.
Summary workflow SmartStudy supports both full-document and chapter summaries. A summary request can specify scope: "full" or scope: "chapter"; chapter requests also provide a chapter reference from the document metadata. Generated results are cached so that later requests can reuse an existing summary unless forceRefresh is requested.
Figure 5.27: Summary endpoints, scopes, and cache behavior.
The web interface presents the generated summary as key points followed by a concise explanation, allowing the learner to review the document before entering the AI study or quiz workflow.
Figure 5.28: Full-document summary displayed in SmartStudy.
Together, the dashboard, management API, and summary result verify the document lifecycle from ready state to learning use.

title: Preparation and Architecture Decisions
content:
Technical prerequisites Before deploying SmartStudy, the development environment requires:
A GitHub repository and a branch workflow for development, staging, and production. Node.js and npm for the React frontend, TypeScript backend, and AWS CDK project. Docker Engine with Docker Compose v2 for the local-first development environment. An AWS account with access to AWS Amplify, Amazon Cognito, Amazon API Gateway, AWS Lambda, Amazon S3, Amazon SQS, Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon CloudWatch, AWS IAM, and AWS CloudFormation. AWS CLI and AWS CDK configured for infrastructure deployment. A self-hosted local AI server running Ollama with Qwen 2.5 7B. Sensitive values must be supplied through deployment environment configuration and must not be committed to GitHub. The project keeps a .env.example file to document required variable names while excluding actual credentials.
AWS Region The SmartStudy staging and production environments are deployed in US East (N. Virginia), us-east-1. Using one Region keeps the AWS resources and CDK stacks consistent across both environments.
Figure 5.3: The AWS Region selected for the SmartStudy deployment.
Infrastructure as Code preparation The infra/ module contains the AWS CDK project. Its structure includes the CDK entry point, reusable stack definitions, tests, TypeScript configuration, and package dependencies. This approach allows staging and production resources to be deployed from the same infrastructure definitions while applying environment-specific names and configuration.
Figure 5.4: AWS CDK project stored in the infra module.
Architecture constraints and changes The initial architecture was revised after validating service access and deployment constraints.
Custom domain The team initially planned to register smartstudylearning.com through Amazon Route 53. Domain registration could not be completed, so the production application retained the default secure amplifyapp.com domain provided by AWS Amplify.
Figure 5.5: Route 53 could not complete the proposed domain registration.
Managed AI service Amazon Bedrock was evaluated as the original managed AI service. The project account received a ValidationException with the message Operation not allowed, so Bedrock was removed from the implemented architecture.
Figure 5.6: Amazon Bedrock access limitation encountered during evaluation.
The final implementation uses Ollama with Qwen 2.5 7B on a self-hosted local AI server. The repository includes a dedicated cloudflare-relay/ component that provides the relay layer between the deployed backend and the local AI service.
Figure 5.7: Cloudflare relay module included in the SmartStudy repository.
Final decisions The following decisions define the implemented project scope:
Use the default AWS Amplify domain instead of Route 53. Use Ollama and Qwen 2.5 7B instead of Amazon Bedrock. Use deployment environment configuration without AWS Secrets Manager. Use Amazon CloudWatch for application monitoring; AWS CloudTrail is outside the implemented scope. Maintain separate staging and production resources. Use AWS CDK and AWS CloudFormation for repeatable infrastructure deployment. With these prerequisites and decisions established, the next section can proceed with the deployment of the core AWS infrastructure.

title: Verify Deployed Resources
content:
Verification approach After the CloudFormation stacks reached a successful state, the resulting resources were verified in the AWS Management Console. The following screenshots show the final deployed state; they are not manual resource-creation steps.
Amazon API Gateway Two Regional HTTP APIs were provisioned, corresponding to the SmartStudy staging and production environments. These APIs provide the public backend entry point used by the deployed frontend.
Figure 5.10: Regional HTTP APIs created for staging and production.
AWS Lambda Six Node.js 22.x Lambda functions were deployed:
An API function for each environment. A Document Ingestion function for each environment. A Pre Sign-up function for each environment. The API functions handle application requests, the ingestion functions process uploaded documents, and the pre-sign-up functions support the Cognito registration workflow.
Figure 5.11: Lambda functions deployed for staging and production.
Amazon S3 The account contains a CDK asset bucket and two SmartStudy document buckets. The staging and production document buckets keep uploaded learning materials separated by environment.
Figure 5.12: CDK asset bucket and environment-specific document buckets.
Amazon SQS The deployed Amazon SQS queues support asynchronous document processing and decouple the user-facing upload request from the longer document-ingestion workload.
Figure 5.13: Amazon SQS queues supporting document processing.
Amazon DynamoDB SmartStudy uses nine logical tables per environment:
AI jobs. Attempts. Conversations. Conversation messages. Document chunks. Documents. Exams. Quizzes. Summaries. The production and staging resources use distinct names so that test data and operations do not affect the production application.
Figure 5.14: Production tables and the first group of staging tables.
Figure 5.15: Remaining staging DynamoDB tables.
Verification result The service inventories match the staging and production resources expected from the SmartStudy foundation stacks. Detailed integration flows—authentication, document processing, AI access, and monitoring—are presented separately in the following workshop sections.

title: Event 1
content:
Report on “Meetup First Cloud Journey” Purpose of the Event Create a platform for networking and knowledge sharing within the technology community. Introduce career pathways from IT Helpdesk and System Administration to Cloud and DevOps. Share knowledge about Cloud Native technologies, Docker, Serverless computing, and real-time architectures on AWS. Present emerging applications of Generative AI, GraphRAG, and Machine Learning. Raise awareness of cybersecurity and cloud security best practices. Share practical principles and techniques for improving teamwork and collaboration. Speakers Trần Trung Vinh – System Administrator at Central Retail Group Việt Phát – AI Student at Swinburne University of Technology Bảo Huỳnh – Junior Cloud Native Developer at Endava Vietnam Trương Phước – Speaker on Effective Teamwork Lê Hoàng Gia Đại – Final-year student at HUTECH University Nguyễn Quốc Bảo – Speaker on Real-time Application Development on AWS Event Photos Key Highlights From IT Helpdesk to Senior System Administrator The first session shared a real-world career journey from an IT Helpdesk position to becoming a Senior System Administrator while providing guidance for transitioning into Cloud and DevOps.
Build a strong foundation in operating systems, networking, and system administration. Develop self-learning habits and proactive problem-solving skills. Gain practical experience through system operations. Gradually learn Cloud technologies, Infrastructure as Code, and DevOps practices. Build a career roadmap based on personal goals and capabilities. The presentation emphasized that transitioning into Cloud and DevOps is a continuous learning process that requires persistence and practical experience.
AWS Neptune for Building a Graph Knowledge Base for GraphRAG This session introduced how to build a graph-based knowledge base using Amazon Neptune together with large language models through Amazon Bedrock.
Amazon Neptune: Graph database for storing and querying highly connected data. Amazon Bedrock: Managed service for accessing foundation models. Graph Knowledge Base: Represents knowledge and relationships between entities. GraphRAG: Combines graph retrieval with Generative AI to improve answer accuracy. Compared to traditional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), GraphRAG leverages relationships between entities to provide more contextual and accurate responses.
Docker – A Containerization Technology This presentation introduced the fundamentals of Docker and containerization.
Package applications together with their dependencies into containers. Ensure consistency across development, testing, and production environments. Reduce deployment issues caused by environment differences. Utilize system resources more efficiently than traditional virtual machines. Simplify application deployment in Cloud Native environments. The speaker also compared containers with virtual machines, highlighting the lightweight nature and faster startup time of containers.
The Art of Effective Teamwork This session focused on improving collaboration and teamwork within software development projects.
Define clear common goals. Assign roles and responsibilities appropriately. Maintain transparent communication. Respect differences among team members. Track project progress using digital collaboration tools. Support teammates whenever challenges arise. The session emphasized that technical knowledge alone is insufficient; effective teamwork is equally important for project success.
Combining AWS WAF with Machine Learning for Cyber Attack Detection on AWS This presentation demonstrated how Machine Learning can be integrated with AWS WAF to detect abnormal network traffic.
AWS WAF: Protects web applications by filtering incoming requests. Machine Learning: Detects suspicious traffic patterns. Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS): Identifies potential cyber attacks. Automated Response: Automatically blocks malicious requests. Combining AWS WAF with Machine Learning enables more intelligent threat detection beyond traditional rule-based filtering.
Multiplayer in the Cloud: Connecting Godot Clients with AWS WebSockets The final session presented a real-time multiplayer game architecture using Godot 4 and AWS Serverless services.
Key components included:
Amazon API Gateway WebSocket API: Maintains bidirectional communication. AWS Lambda: Handles connection, disconnection, and messaging events. Amazon DynamoDB: Stores player and connection information. Godot Client: Communicates with the backend through WebSockets. This Serverless architecture eliminates server management while automatically scaling based on the number of active connections.
Knowledge Gained Career Development Career growth from IT Helpdesk to Cloud Engineer or DevOps Engineer requires a clear learning roadmap. Self-learning and problem-solving are essential skills in the IT industry. Strong fundamentals in Linux, networking, and system administration remain valuable when moving into Cloud. Combining theoretical knowledge with practical projects accelerates professional growth. Cloud Native Architecture Docker provides consistent application environments. Containers form the foundation of modern Cloud Native applications. Serverless computing reduces operational overhead while enabling automatic scaling. WebSockets are suitable for real-time communication systems. DynamoDB can efficiently manage connection states in Serverless architectures. Generative AI and GraphRAG GraphRAG combines knowledge retrieval with large language models. Amazon Neptune is well suited for highly connected datasets. Amazon Bedrock simplifies the integration of foundation models. Graph-based knowledge improves AI response accuracy and contextual understanding. Cloud Security AWS WAF helps protect web applications from malicious requests. Machine Learning improves anomaly detection capabilities. Security should be incorporated during the system design phase. Monitoring and automated responses reduce incident response time. Teamwork Skills Clearly defined responsibilities improve project efficiency. Regular communication minimizes misunderstandings. Project management tools simplify task tracking. Mutual respect and collaboration contribute significantly to successful projects. Practical Applications Career Planning: Continue improving Linux, networking, AWS, Docker, and DevOps skills. Docker Adoption: Containerize applications to ensure consistent deployment environments. GraphRAG Research: Explore combining Amazon Bedrock with Amazon Neptune for intelligent question-answering systems. Security Enhancement: Study AWS WAF, CloudWatch, and anomaly detection techniques. Real-time Systems: Experiment with API Gateway WebSocket, Lambda, and DynamoDB for real-time applications. Team Collaboration: Apply project management tools to improve communication and task management. Event Experience Participating in Meetup First Cloud Journey was an enriching experience because the event covered a broad range of modern technologies instead of focusing on a single topic.
Learning from Experienced Speakers The career-sharing session provided valuable insights into professional growth within the IT industry. The speakers emphasized the importance of continuous learning and practical experience when transitioning to Cloud and DevOps. Their experiences motivated me to establish a clearer learning roadmap for my future career. Exploring Emerging Technologies The GraphRAG session introduced a practical application of Generative AI beyond conversational chatbots. Learning how Amazon Neptune integrates with Amazon Bedrock expanded my understanding of knowledge-driven AI systems. The AWS WAF and Machine Learning presentation demonstrated how AI can strengthen cloud security. Understanding Cloud Native and Serverless The Docker presentation clarified the role of containers in modern software development. The WebSocket session illustrated how real-time applications can be built without managing servers. The Serverless architecture using API Gateway, Lambda, and DynamoDB can be extended to chat systems, notifications, and live collaboration platforms. Improving Teamwork Skills The teamwork session highlighted that technical expertise alone does not guarantee project success. Effective communication, proper task allocation, and continuous progress tracking are equally important. These principles can be applied directly to collaborative university projects and the SmartStudy AI project. Networking and Knowledge Sharing The event provided opportunities to learn from professionals working in different technology domains. The presentations broadened my understanding of Cloud computing, AI, cybersecurity, and software architecture. Participating in community meetups also encouraged continuous learning and professional networking. Key Takeaways Continuous self-learning is essential for long-term career development. GraphRAG demonstrates how Generative AI can leverage graph databases for more intelligent information retrieval. Machine Learning can significantly improve cloud security solutions. title: Blog 3
content:
HOSTING A .NET BLAZOR WEBASSEMBLY APPLICATION ON AMAZON S3 AND AMAZON CLOUDFRONT Blazor WebAssembly is a Microsoft framework that enables developers to build interactive web applications using C# instead of JavaScript. The application runs directly in the browser through WebAssembly technology. After publishing, it generates a collection of static files, including HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and .NET runtime libraries, making it suitable for deployment on static website hosting services.
The AWS .NET Blog demonstrates how to deploy a Blazor WebAssembly application using Amazon S3 together with Amazon CloudFront. Amazon S3 serves as the storage location for all published application files, while Amazon CloudFront acts as a Content Delivery Network (CDN) that improves website performance by reducing latency and delivering content through AWS Edge Locations worldwide. In addition, the deployment process utilizes Terraform to automate the provisioning of AWS infrastructure.
Figure 1. Deployment architecture of a Blazor WebAssembly application using Amazon S3 and Amazon CloudFront. Main Components of the System Blazor WebAssembly
Blazor WebAssembly is the client-side application developed with .NET. After executing the dotnet publish command, the application is compiled into static files that are ready for deployment.
Amazon S3
Amazon S3 is used to host all published website files. It provides high scalability, excellent durability, and cost-effective storage for static web applications.
Amazon CloudFront
Amazon CloudFront functions as a Content Delivery Network (CDN), distributing website content through Edge Locations around the world. This significantly reduces latency and improves page loading performance.
Terraform
Terraform is used to automatically provision AWS resources such as Amazon S3 buckets, CloudFront distributions, and other required infrastructure. By adopting Infrastructure as Code (IaC), deployments become faster, more consistent, and easier to maintain.
Benefits of the Architecture Combining Amazon S3 with Amazon CloudFront provides several advantages:
Faster website loading through a global CDN. Automatic scalability to handle increasing traffic. Low deployment cost without managing servers. Easy application deployment and updates. Infrastructure automation using Terraform. Deploying the Application on AWS After publishing the Blazor WebAssembly application, the next step is to deploy it to AWS.
Step 1: Publish the Application Use the following command to generate the Release version of the application.
dotnet publish -c Release After publishing, all website files are generated in the following directory:
bin/Release/net9.0/publish/wwwroot This directory contains the static files that will be uploaded to Amazon S3.
Step 2: Prepare Terraform Clone the Terraform sample project provided by AWS.
git clone https://github.com/aws-samples/aws-blazor-hosting.git cd aws-blazor-hosting Initialize Terraform.
terraform init Step 3: Deploy the Infrastructure After configuring the required variables, deploy the infrastructure using:
terraform apply Terraform automatically provisions the following AWS resources:
Amazon S3 Bucket Amazon CloudFront Distribution Origin Access Control (OAC) Bucket Policy Supporting infrastructure resources Once the deployment is complete, Terraform returns the CloudFront domain name, which can be used to access the application.
Step 4: Upload the Website to Amazon S3 Synchronize the published website files to Amazon S3 using the AWS CLI.
aws s3 sync ./publish/wwwroot s3://your-bucket-name After the upload is completed, Amazon CloudFront distributes the website content to end users.
Step 5: Verify the Deployment Once the CloudFront distribution status changes to Deployment Completed, access the CloudFront domain name to verify that the website is running correctly.
Whenever the application is updated, simply publish the project again and synchronize the new files to Amazon S3.
Accelerating Website Performance with CloudFront CloudFront stores cached copies of website content across multiple Edge Locations worldwide. When users access the website, the requested content is delivered from the nearest Edge Location instead of directly from Amazon S3.
This provides several benefits:
Reduced latency. Faster page loading. Lower workload on Amazon S3. Improved user experience. Future Enhancements After successfully deploying the application, the architecture can be extended with additional AWS services, including:
AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) for HTTPS support. Amazon Route 53 for custom domain names. AWS WAF for enhanced security. GitHub Actions or AWS CodePipeline for automated deployments. Amazon CloudWatch for monitoring and logging. Advantages and Limitations Advantages No server management required. Low deployment cost. High website performance through CloudFront. Automatic scalability based on traffic. Infrastructure automation with Terraform. Easy application updates. Limitations Suitable only for static websites and client-side applications. CloudFront invalidation may be required after updates to refresh cached content immediately. Proper configuration of Bucket Policies and Origin Access Control (OAC) is necessary to maintain security. Conclusion Deploying a Blazor WebAssembly application on Amazon S3 together with Amazon CloudFront provides an efficient serverless solution for hosting modern web applications. Amazon S3 is responsible for storing the website files, while CloudFront accelerates content delivery through AWS’s global CDN network. Furthermore, Terraform automates infrastructure provisioning, making deployments faster, more consistent, and easier to manage.
Reference
https://aws.amazon.com/vi/blogs/dotnet/host-a-net-blazor-webassembly-app-on-amazon-s3-and-amazon-cloudfront/

title: Amazon Cognito Authentication
content:
User pools Amazon Cognito provides the authentication layer for the deployed SmartStudy application. Two single-Region User Pools were created in us-east-1, corresponding to the staging and production environments.
Figure 5.21: Separate Amazon Cognito User Pools for SmartStudy environments.
The authentication workflow is:
The user submits registration or sign-in information through the React frontend. Amazon Cognito validates the request and manages the user identity. The Pre Sign-up Lambda can apply project-specific registration logic. After successful authentication, Cognito issues tokens to the client. The frontend includes the access token when calling protected backend operations. Separate User Pools prevent staging users and configuration from affecting production authentication. Passwords and authentication credentials are handled by Cognito and are not stored directly by the frontend.

title: Core AWS Infrastructure
content:
Overview SmartStudy uses Infrastructure as Code instead of creating each AWS resource manually in the AWS Management Console. The infrastructure is defined in TypeScript with AWS CDK, synthesized into AWS CloudFormation templates, and deployed as separate staging and production stacks.
AWS CDK source │ synthesize and deploy ▼ AWS CloudFormation ├── SmartStudy-staging-Foundation └── SmartStudy-production-Foundation ├── Amazon API Gateway ├── AWS Lambda ├── Amazon S3 ├── Amazon SQS processing queues ├── Amazon DynamoDB └── Supporting IAM and monitoring resources Because deployment is automated, this workshop documents the reproducible CDK workflow and verifies the resources created by CloudFormation. It does not recreate console wizards or present the final resource lists as manual creation steps.
Content Deploy infrastructure with AWS CDK and CloudFormation Verify deployed AWS resources Review IAM roles and policies The deployment evidence is organized into three layers: the CDK source definition, successful CloudFormation stacks, and the resulting AWS resources in staging and production.

title: IAM Roles and Policies
content:
IAM role separation SmartStudy uses AWS IAM roles to separate infrastructure-deployment permissions from application runtime permissions. AWS CDK creates bootstrap roles for CloudFormation execution, deployment, asset publishing, and context lookup. The SmartStudy stacks create additional execution roles for the Lambda functions in each environment.
The role inventory confirms the presence of:
CDK CloudFormation execution and deployment roles. CDK file-publishing, image-publishing, and lookup roles. API Lambda execution roles for staging and production. Document Ingestion Lambda execution roles for staging and production. Pre Sign-up Lambda execution roles for staging and production. The Lambda service is listed as the trusted entity for the SmartStudy execution roles, while AWS CloudFormation is trusted by the CDK CloudFormation execution role.
Figure 5.16: CDK deployment roles and SmartStudy Lambda execution roles.
Customer-managed policies The IAM policy inventory includes customer-managed policies associated with the SmartStudy API Lambda roles in staging and production. It also contains a Lambda basic-execution policy used to support runtime logging. Some policies generated by CDK use an overflow-policy name when the permissions cannot remain entirely within the primary inline policy.
Figure 5.17: Customer-managed policies, including policies generated for SmartStudy API functions.
These screenshots verify the role and policy separation at a high level. They do not expose or claim every individual IAM action. Detailed permissions remain defined by the CDK source and should follow the principle of least privilege: each function receives only the access required for its own workflow.
Security result The IAM structure provides:
Separate deployment and runtime trust boundaries. Environment-specific Lambda execution roles. Service-based trust relationships rather than long-lived application credentials. Repeatable role and policy creation through AWS CDK and CloudFormation. Account identifiers in the evidence images are redacted before publication.

title: Published Blog Posts
content:
Blog 1 - Building an AI Gateway for Amazon Bedrock with Amazon API Gateway This blog introduces the AI Gateway architecture on AWS, which uses Amazon API Gateway as an intermediary layer between applications and Amazon Bedrock to centrally manage access to foundation models. It explains the roles of core AWS services, including Amazon API Gateway, AWS Lambda, IAM, and Amazon Bedrock, while analyzing the request flow, security benefits, scalability, cost control, and the deployment process using AWS CloudFormation.
Blog 2 - Deploying an AI Gateway for Amazon Bedrock on AWS This blog provides a step-by-step guide to deploying an AI Gateway on AWS using AWS CloudFormation. It covers environment preparation, infrastructure deployment, gateway validation, Response Streaming, and potential enhancements such as AWS WAF, Amazon CloudWatch, API Versioning, and Canary Deployment to improve security, monitoring, and scalability.
Blog 3 - Hosting a .NET Blazor WebAssembly Application on Amazon S3 and Amazon CloudFront This blog demonstrates how to deploy a .NET Blazor WebAssembly application using a serverless architecture with Amazon S3 and Amazon CloudFront. It explains the roles of Blazor WebAssembly, Amazon S3, Amazon CloudFront, and Terraform, and provides guidance on publishing the application, provisioning infrastructure, uploading the website to Amazon S3, and improving website performance through CloudFront’s global content delivery network.

title: Frontend Deployment and Authentication
content:
Overview SmartStudy delivers its React frontend through AWS Amplify and authenticates users with Amazon Cognito. The deployment does not use a custom VPC, subnet, NAT Gateway, or Internet Gateway. Amplify, Cognito, API Gateway, and Lambda are used as managed regional services.
The delivery and authentication flow is:
GitHub branch │ push / merge ▼ AWS Amplify Hosting │ ├── Amazon Cognito: registration, sign-in, and tokens └── Amazon API Gateway: authenticated backend requests Content GitHub branch workflow AWS Amplify deployment Amazon Cognito authentication Authentication result

title: Document Processing
content:
Overview SmartStudy processes uploaded PDF documents asynchronously so that long-running extraction and chunking tasks do not block the user-facing API. The application follows the same business workflow in local and AWS environments through replaceable infrastructure adapters.
React frontend │ request a presigned upload URL ▼ API Gateway → API Lambda │ ├── metadata → DynamoDB └── presigned URL → browser uploads PDF → Amazon S3 │ upload completed ▼ Amazon SQS │ ▼ Document Ingestion Lambda ├── extract PDF text ├── detect headings ├── split document chunks └── persist results in DynamoDB The deployed workflow uses document storage, an Amazon SQS processing queue, a document-ingestion function, and document-related tables.
Content Upload and process documents Manage and summarize documents

title: Workshop
content:
Build and Deploy SmartStudy AI Overview This workshop documents the implemented SmartStudy platform from source organization to deployed AWS resources and application results. SmartStudy follows a local-first Ports & Adapters design and uses serverless AWS services for its deployed frontend, authentication, APIs, storage, asynchronous processing, persistence, and monitoring.
The implemented architecture does not use a custom VPC, subnet, NAT Gateway, Internet Gateway, Route 53 domain, Amazon Bedrock, AWS Secrets Manager, or AWS CloudTrail. AI capabilities are provided by Ollama with Qwen 2.5 7B on a self-hosted local AI server through a Cloudflare relay component.
Current content SmartStudy overview Preparation and architecture decisions Deploy and verify core AWS infrastructure Deploy the frontend and configure authentication Upload, process, and manage documents Integrate Ollama and the AI study workflow Validate monitoring and project results The final sections document AI integration, monitoring, end-to-end results, and the operating status through the evaluation period.

title: Self-assessment
content:
Throughout my internship, I had the opportunity to explore and work with a wide range of services on the Amazon Web Services (AWS) platform while actively participating in the development and deployment of the SmartStudy AI project. This internship enabled me to apply the knowledge I had learned at university to real-world scenarios and gain hands-on experience in developing and deploying cloud-based applications.
Through the project, I gained practical experience with AWS services such as AWS Amplify, Amazon Cognito, Amazon API Gateway, AWS Lambda, Amazon S3, Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon SQS, and Amazon CloudWatch. In addition, I had the opportunity to study serverless architecture, infrastructure deployment using AWS CloudFormation and AWS CDK, as well as integrating a locally hosted AI model into the system through Cloudflare Relay.
During the internship, I consistently strived to complete assigned tasks on schedule, proactively studied the official AWS documentation, and collaborated with team members whenever I encountered challenges. This experience not only strengthened my technical knowledge but also improved my teamwork, self-learning, analytical thinking, and problem-solving skills in a professional working environment.

title: AI Integration
content:
Overview SmartStudy uses Ollama with Qwen 2.5 7B as its AI provider. The model runs on a self-hosted local AI server rather than Amazon Bedrock. A dedicated Cloudflare relay component connects the deployed backend workflow to the local AI service.
SmartStudy frontend │ ▼ API Gateway → API Lambda │ AI request with retrieved context ▼ Cloudflare relay │ ▼ Self-hosted local AI server Ollama + Qwen 2.5 7B │ generated response └──────────────► SmartStudy The AI layer is used for document-grounded study assistance, summaries, quiz generation, and answer explanations. Document storage and retrieval remain separate from the model host.
Content Ollama and Cloudflare relay AI study room

title: Feedback and Suggestions
content:
This section summarizes my personal experience throughout the First Cloud AI Journey program and provides suggestions that may help improve the learning experience for future participants.
Overall Evaluation 1. Learning Environment The learning environment was well organized, supportive, and highly motivating. The program provided a clear learning roadmap that gradually introduced AWS services before moving on to real project implementation. The combination of self-learning materials, workshops, and hands-on practice created an effective learning experience.
2. Mentor and Community Support The mentors and community members were always willing to answer questions and provide guidance whenever difficulties arose. Instead of simply giving solutions, they encouraged participants to analyze problems independently and explore the AWS documentation. This approach helped me become more confident in troubleshooting and problem-solving.
3. Relevance to My Academic Background The program was closely aligned with my university major in Information Technology. It allowed me to apply theoretical knowledge in software development while learning practical cloud computing concepts, serverless architecture, and AWS best practices that are not always covered in university courses.
4. Learning Opportunities One of the most valuable aspects of the program was the opportunity to build a complete cloud application instead of only completing isolated labs. Developing the SmartStudy AI project allowed me to gain practical experience with AWS Amplify, Amazon Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, S3, DynamoDB, SQS, CloudWatch, CloudFormation, and AWS CDK while also improving my documentation and technical writing skills.
5. Community and Collaboration The program fostered a collaborative atmosphere where participants were encouraged to share ideas and help one another. Discussions within the community made learning more engaging and allowed me to learn from the experiences of other participants.
6. Overall Program Experience Overall, I believe the First Cloud AI Journey program successfully bridges the gap between academic learning and practical cloud development. The combination of guided learning, real projects, technical blogs, and workshops provided a comprehensive learning experience that strengthened both my technical knowledge and practical skills.
Additional Questions What was the most satisfying part of the program? The most rewarding experience was building and deploying the SmartStudy AI project on AWS. Seeing the application evolve from an initial idea into a working cloud-based system gave me a deeper understanding of AWS services and increased my confidence in designing and deploying cloud applications.
What could be improved? It would be beneficial to include more advanced workshops focusing on topics such as CI/CD, Infrastructure as Code, cloud security best practices, and cost optimization. Additional sessions covering troubleshooting techniques and real-world deployment scenarios would also help participants gain a deeper understanding of production environments.
Would you recommend this program to your friends? Yes. I would definitely recommend the First Cloud AI Journey program to students who want to learn AWS through practical experience. The program provides a structured learning path, opportunities to work on real projects, and valuable guidance from experienced mentors, making it an excellent starting point for anyone interested in cloud computing.
Suggestions and Expectations Continue updating the learning materials to include newly released AWS services and features. Organize additional technical sharing sessions where participants can present their projects and exchange experiences. Provide more real-world project scenarios that simulate enterprise environments. Include optional advanced learning tracks for participants who want to explore cloud architecture, DevOps, AI, or security in greater depth. Maintain and further expand the supportive learning community, as it is one of the program’s greatest strengths. Overall, I am grateful for the opportunity to participate in the First Cloud AI Journey program. The knowledge, practical experience, and confidence I gained throughout the program will serve as a strong foundation for my future career in cloud computing and software engineering.

title: Cost Analysis
content:
Operational Cost Analysis The cost data was collected from AWS Cost Explorer for services included in the SmartStudy architecture in the US East (N. Virginia) Region. It represents the development, testing, and demonstration period with low usage volume.
Cost by service Service Recorded cost (USD) Active period AWS Lambda $0.0000419621 8 days Amazon S3 $0.0000003024 13 days AWS Amplify ≈ $0.0000000001 8 days Amazon CloudWatch $0.00 19 days Amazon SQS $0.00 8 days Amazon Cognito $0.00 6 days Amazon API Gateway $0.00 8 days AWS CloudFormation $0.00 8 days Amazon DynamoDB ≈ $0.00 8 days The total directly recorded positive cost for this period was approximately $0.0000422646, equivalent to $0.00 when rounded to the nearest cent.
Very small billing adjustments and negative values are not treated as operational service costs and were excluded from the table to avoid confusion. Services that are not part of the final SmartStudy architecture were also excluded from the calculation.
Assessment The recorded cost was extremely low because the system was used only for development and demonstration with a limited number of users, requests, and documents. Most services recorded no charge during the measured period.
These results represent the demonstration environment and should not be considered a production cost forecast. Costs will change as the number of users, documents, API requests, storage duration, and log volume increase. The self-hosted local AI server running Ollama is outside AWS Cost Explorer and is therefore not included in the AWS cost table.
AWS promotional credits are applied at the account level. Credits reduce the amount payable but do not change the amount of AWS resources consumed by SmartStudy.
